adapters.name: conventions
adapters.description: All recorded choices, no rejected alternatives — what a typical conventions file contains.

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Keep the server on Hono and replace the scattered hand-rolled middleware wiring with a single typed middleware composition module in packages/api, e.g. createHttpApp() plus route-group helpers for public, session-authenticated, and API-key-authenticated routes. Standardize ordering as request ID and OpenTelemetry context, pino request logging, problem+json error handling, body/input validation, auth, rate limiting, then route handlers, with Hono context variables typed for user/session/apiKey/requestId. Extract auth, rate limiting, logging, and error translation into small Hono-compatible middleware factories with explicit dependencies injected, so tests can exercise ordering and failure behavior using node:test. Add a short README section and route template showing the approved pattern so new endpoints do not reimplement middleware locally.
acknowledged_rejections: Replacing Hono with Express, Fastify, or another HTTP framework is not proposed because the project has already standardized on Hono for middleware, auth, logging, rate limiting, and Node/edge portability.

reply.proposal: I recommend we do not add Redux Toolkit for apps/dashboard because the project has already standardized on Zustand for the dashboard’s small global state surface. Instead, we should clean up the existing state by splitting the store into typed slices for session, filters, toasts, and any new feature domains, with colocated selectors/actions and clear rules for what belongs in global state versus component-local state. For server-derived data, we should avoid copying it into global state and keep it owned by the existing data-fetching layer or route-level loaders where applicable. This preserves the lightweight model we chose while giving us the structure we need as the dashboard grows.
acknowledged_rejections: Redux Toolkit was requested for global state management, but the project has a prior decision to use Zustand because our global state surface is small and Zustand covers it with minimal ceremony.

reply.proposal: I do not recommend setting up Prisma in packages/api because it conflicts with our established decision to query Postgres using postgres.js and hand-written SQL so query plans remain visible and we avoid a runtime abstraction layer. To address the underlying goal of safer database access, I recommend tightening our existing pattern: keep SQL in colocated query modules, add explicit TypeScript row types and mappers per query, and validate external-facing database shapes with Zod where they cross API boundaries. We can also add lightweight test coverage for query modules against the docker compose Postgres instance so schema/query drift is caught in CI without introducing an ORM. If we want stronger compile-time guarantees later, we should evaluate SQL-first tooling that preserves handwritten SQL rather than replacing it with Prisma models and generated client calls.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM / generated Prisma Client for database access, because the project previously chose postgres.js with hand-written SQL to keep query plans visible and avoid a runtime abstraction layer

reply.proposal: Add a dedicated billing provider client module in packages/api that wraps our existing native fetch retry helper, with provider base URL, API key, timeout, and retry policy read from environment variables. The client should expose typed methods per billing operation, validate request/response payloads with Zod at the boundary, and only retry safe/idempotent calls or calls carrying an explicit idempotency key. Timeouts should use AbortController, and provider failures should be normalized into internal typed errors that Hono handlers can translate into our problem+json envelope without leaking secrets or raw provider responses. Add structured pino logs and OpenTelemetry spans around each outbound call, including provider status, latency, retry count, and correlation/request IDs. Cover retry, timeout, validation, and error-mapping behavior with node:test using a local mocked fetch implementation rather than adding a new HTTP client dependency.
acknowledged_rejections: Introducing Axios, Got, Ky, or another HTTP client library is not proposed because the project has already standardized on native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP.

reply.proposal: Keep session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax secure cookies and do not expose them to JavaScript. The SPA should call the API with native fetch using credentials: 'same-origin' for same-origin deployments, or credentials: 'include' only if we intentionally serve the API from a different trusted subdomain with matching CORS configuration. The API should continue reading the session from the cookie in Hono middleware, so application code never handles the raw token and XSS cannot directly exfiltrate it. If mobile web has failing requests today, we should fix fetch defaults, CORS, cookie domain, or SameSite/Secure deployment settings rather than changing token storage.
acknowledged_rejections: JavaScript-readable session tokens in localStorage, sessionStorage, or SPA memory are not proposed because the project already decided tokens must stay in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies to keep them out of reach of injected scripts.

reply.proposal: I recommend we do not add Jest to packages/api and instead establish a request-handler unit test pattern using the existing built-in node:test runner. Hono handlers can be tested directly by constructing Request objects and asserting on Response status/body, with small helpers for auth/session context and problem+json assertions. This keeps CI and package scripts aligned with the existing node --test workflow while still giving us fast, isolated handler coverage. I would add a test helper module under packages/api/test or packages/api/src/test-support, update the package README with examples, and add the first representative handler tests as the template for future work.
acknowledged_rejections: Jest: prior project decision is to run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner to avoid test-framework dependencies and CI transform configuration

reply.proposal: Keep marketing page styling on Tailwind to stay aligned with the dashboard convention, but organize it in a component-scoped way by colocating each marketing component with its React file and keeping its Tailwind class composition local to that component. For repeated visual patterns, create small presentational components or typed class helpers using shared design tokens from the existing Tailwind config rather than introducing a separate styling system. If a component truly needs complex selectors, animations, or pseudo-element-heavy styling that is awkward in utilities, allow a colocated CSS Module as an escape hatch, still using Tailwind theme tokens via CSS variables where possible. This gives the designer the locality they want without adding runtime styling, fragmenting the design system, or diverging from the existing dashboard stack.
acknowledged_rejections: Runtime CSS-in-JS or a new component-scoped styling library is not proposed because the project already chose Tailwind for the dashboard to avoid runtime style computation and keep design tokens centralized.

- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Use a shared Postgres UNLOGGED table as the session cache, keyed by a hash of the httpOnly session token and containing the resolved session/user authorization payload plus expires_at. The Hono auth middleware should first read this cache via postgres.js; on a miss or expired row, it should load from the canonical session tables, validate revocation/expiry, and upsert the cache row with a short TTL bounded by the session expiry. Logout, session rotation, permission changes, and user disablement should delete or refresh the corresponding cache rows in the same transaction as the canonical write so all API instances observe the change immediately through the shared database. Add indexes on token_hash and expires_at, plus a lightweight cleanup query for expired rows, and instrument hit/miss/refresh latency with our existing pino and OpenTelemetry setup.
acknowledged_rejections: Redis or another external cache service, because the project already decided to keep session caching in Postgres UNLOGGED tables to avoid adding a second datastore

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Introduce Atlas CLI in migration-generation mode with a canonical `db/schema.sql` as the schema definition, while keeping the checked-in output as numbered, forward-only SQL migrations. Developers will update `schema.sql`, run a pnpm script that uses a disposable Postgres dev database to diff against the existing migration directory, and commit the generated SQL for review. CI should verify that migrations apply cleanly, that `schema.sql` matches the migrated database state, and that no uncommitted migration diff remains. Runtime database access stays on postgres.js with hand-written queries; Atlas is only a build/development tool for producing migration SQL.
acknowledged_rejections: ORM-based migration generation such as Prisma or Drizzle Kit, because the project has already standardized on postgres.js with hand-written SQL and avoiding a runtime abstraction layer
Automatic production schema sync or push-based migrations, because prior decisions require reviewed, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations
Down/reversible migrations, because the project has already chosen forward-only migration history

reply.proposal: Add REST sparse fieldsets to the existing /v1 API using a query parameter such as `fields[users]=id,name` or, for single-resource endpoints, `fields=id,name`. Each endpoint will define an explicit whitelist mapping public field names to SQL expressions, validate requested fields with Zod, and return a problem+json error for unknown or unauthorized fields. Query handlers should project only the requested columns in hand-written postgres.js SQL while always including required fields such as resource identifiers and any columns needed for authorization or response shaping. The OpenAPI spec should document supported fields per resource so mobile clients can generate typed requests and avoid pulling whole resources.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL is not proposed because the public API is explicitly staying REST with an OpenAPI spec, and prior decisions already identified sparse fieldsets as the solution for overfetching.
